entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

field.name: content
field.text: lets cook something for 5 persons. Ask me questions and use all the available functions to propose

field.name: lines
field.text: checkpoints("cooking_for_5", "Plan and prepare a meal for 5 people using all available functions", [

field.name: lines
field.text: { id: "gather_preferences", instructions: "Ask user about cuisine, dietary restrictions, and cooking preferences", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } },

field.name: lines
field.text: { id: "load_knowledge", instructions: "Load relevant knowledge from cooking and nutrition spaces", outputSchema: { loaded: { type: "boolean" } } },

field.name: lines
field.text: { id: "create_recipe", instructions: "Create a recipe proposal for 5 people", outputSchema: { recipe: { type: "object" } } },

field.name: lines
field.text: { id: "calculate_nutrition", instructions: "Calculate nutrition information for the meal", outputSchema: { nutrition: { type: "object" } } },

field.name: lines
field.text: { id: "build_grocery_list", instructions: "Build a grocery list for the meal", outputSchema: { groceries: { type: "array" } } },

field.name: lines
field.text: { id: "present_results", instructions: "Display all results using display components", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }

field.name: description
field.text: Plan and prepare a meal for 5 people using all available functions

field.name: instructions
field.text: Ask user about cuisine, dietary restrictions, and cooking preferences

field.name: instructions
field.text: Load relevant knowledge from cooking and nutrition spaces

field.name: instructions
field.text: Create a recipe proposal for 5 people

field.name: instructions
field.text: Calculate nutrition information for the meal

field.name: instructions
field.text: Build a grocery list for the meal

field.name: instructions
field.text: Display all results using display components

field.name: lines
field.text: <Select name="cuisine" label="What cuisine would you like?" options={["italian", "japanese", "mexican"]} />

entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

field.name: content
field.text: checkpoints("cooking_for_5", "Plan and prepare a meal for 5 people using all available functions", [ { id: "gather_preferences", instructions: "Ask user about cuisine, dietary restrictions, and cooking preferences", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "load_knowledge", instructions: "Load relevant knowledge from cooking and nutrition spaces", outputSchema: { loaded: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "create_recipe", instructions: "Create a recipe proposal for 5 people", outputSchema: { recipe: { type: "object" } } }, { id: "calculate_nutrition", instructions: "Calculate nutrition information for the meal", outputSchema: { nutrition: { type: "object" } } }, { id: "build_grocery_list", instructions: "Build a grocery list for the meal", outputSchema: { groceries: { type: "array" } } }, { id: "present_results", instructions: "Display all results using display components", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ]) var preferences = await ask(

field.name: japanese
field.text:
# Japanese Cuisine
Japanese cooking is defined by precision, respect for ingredients, and the pursuit of umami — the fifth taste.
## Key Principles - **Umami layering** — Build depth by combining dashi, soy sauce, miso, and mirin in careful ratios. - **Shun (旬)** — Cook with ingredients at their seasonal peak. The menu follows the calendar. - **Presentation** — Food is arranged with intention. Odd numbers, negative space, and color contrast are valued. - **Knife work** — Clean, precise cuts affect texture, cooking time, and appearance equally.
## Essential Techniques - **Dashi making** — Steep kombu and bonito flakes briefly for a clean, umami-rich stock. Never boil the kombu. - **Tempura frying** — Ice-cold batter, high heat (170–180°C), small batches. Lumps in the batter are fine — overmixing makes it heavy. - **Rice washing** — Rinse until the water runs clear, then soak 30 minutes before cooking. - **Teriyaki glaze** — Equal parts soy sauce, mirin, and sake, reduced until glossy.
## Staple Ingredients Short-grain rice, soy sauce, mirin, sake, rice vinegar, miso paste, kombu, bonito flakes, nori, sesame oil, wasabi, ginger.
## Classic Dishes Sushi, ramen, tempura, tonkatsu, miso soup, okonomiyaki, gyudon, matcha desserts.

field.name: vegetarian
field.text:
# Vegetarian Cooking
Vegetarian cooking excludes meat and fish but includes dairy, eggs, and honey.
## Substitution Strategies - **Protein sources** — Eggs, cheese, legumes (lentils, chickpeas, black beans), tofu, tempeh, seitan, nuts - **Umami replacements** — Soy sauce, miso paste, nutritional yeast, aged cheeses, mushrooms, tomato paste - **Stock** — Use vegetable stock or mushroom stock instead of chicken/beef stock - **Gelatin** — Replace with agar-agar (1:1 ratio, but boil to activate)
## Tips - Roast vegetables at high heat (220°C+) for caramelization and depth - Use smoked paprika, liquid smoke, or charred vegetables for smoky notes usually provided by meat - Don't treat vegetarian food as "meat minus the meat" — build dishes around vegetables as the star

field.name: saute
field.text:
# Sauté
High-heat, quick cooking in a wide pan with a small amount of fat. The word comes from French "sauter" — to jump.
## When to Use - Vegetables that should stay crisp-tender (bell peppers, snap peas, asparagus) - Thin cuts of meat or fish (chicken cutlets, shrimp, scallops) - Finishing a dish with a pan sauce after searing
## Key Technique 1. **Heat the pan first**, then add oil. The oil should shimmer but not smoke. 2. **Don't overcrowd** — ingredients should sit in a single layer with space between them. Work in batches if needed. 3. **Keep things moving** — toss or stir frequently. The goal is even browning without burning. 4. **Season at the end** — salt draws out moisture and can prevent browning.
## Equipment A wide, flat-bottomed skillet or sauté pan. Stainless steel gives the best fond (browned bits) for pan sauces. Non-stick works for delicate items like fish.
## Common Mistakes - Starting with a cold pan (food steams instead of searing) - Adding too much food at once (temperature drops, food boils) - Moving food too often when you want a sear (let it sit for 2–3 minutes)

field.name: protein
field.text:
# Protein
Protein is made up of amino acids — 9 of which are essential and must come from food.
## Daily Targets - **Sedentary adults** — 0.8 g per kg of body weight - **Active individuals** — 1.2–1.6 g/kg - **Strength athletes** — 1.6–2.2 g/kg
## Complete vs Incomplete - **Complete proteins** contain all 9 essential amino acids: meat, fish, eggs, dairy, soy, quinoa. - **Incomplete proteins** lack one or more: beans, lentils, grains, nuts. Combine throughout the day to cover all amino acids.
## Top Sources (per 100 g) | Food | Protein | |------|---------| | Chicken breast | 31 g | | Greek yogurt | 10 g | | Lentils (cooked) | 9 g | | Eggs (2 large) | 12 g | | Tofu (firm) | 17 g |
## Timing Spreading protein across 3–4 meals (20–40 g each) maximizes muscle protein synthesis better than loading it into one meal.

field.name: carbohydrates
field.text:
# Carbohydrates
Carbohydrates are the body's preferred fuel source, especially for the brain and high-intensity exercise.
## Types - **Simple carbs** — Quick-digesting sugars (glucose, fructose, sucrose). Found in fruit, honey, table sugar. - **Complex carbs** — Longer chains that digest slowly, providing steady energy. Found in whole grains, legumes, starchy vegetables. - **Fiber** — Indigestible carbs that support gut health and satiety. Soluble fiber (oats, beans) lowers cholesterol; insoluble fiber (whole wheat, vegetables) aids digestion.
## Daily Targets - **General** — 45–65% of total calories (225–325 g on a 2000 kcal diet) - **Endurance athletes** — 6–10 g/kg body weight - **Low-carb approaches** — 50–150 g/day depending on the protocol
## Glycemic Index - **Low GI (< 55)** — Lentils, most fruits, steel-cut oats. Steady blood sugar. - **Medium GI (56–69)** — Brown rice, whole wheat bread, sweet potato. - **High GI (> 70)** — White bread, white rice, potatoes. Fast energy but rapid blood sugar spike.
## Top Sources Oats, brown rice, sweet potatoes, quinoa, beans, whole-grain bread, bananas, berries.

field.name: fats
field.text:
# Fats
Dietary fat is calorie-dense (9 kcal/g vs 4 kcal/g for protein and carbs) but essential for hormone production, brain function, and absorbing fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
## Types - **Unsaturated (healthy)** — Monounsaturated (olive oil, avocado) and polyunsaturated (fish, walnuts, flaxseed). Lower inflammation and support heart health. - **Saturated** — Solid at room temperature (butter, coconut oil, red meat fat). Limit to < 10% of calories. - **Trans fats** — Industrially hydrogenated oils. Avoid entirely — linked to heart disease. - **Omega-3 fatty acids** — EPA and DHA (fatty fish) and ALA (flaxseed, chia). Anti-inflammatory, critical for brain health. Aim for 2 servings of fatty fish per week.
## Daily Targets - **General** — 20–35% of total calories (44–78 g on a 2000 kcal diet) - **Minimum** — Do not go below 15% of calories; hormonal disruption can occur.
## Top Sources Olive oil, avocado, salmon, sardines, walnuts, almonds, chia seeds, eggs, dark chocolate (70%+).

field.name: plate-method
field.text:
# The Plate Method
A simple, no-counting approach to balanced meals. Divide your plate visually:
## The Template - **1/2 plate — Non-starchy vegetables** — Leafy greens, broccoli, peppers, tomatoes, zucchini, cauliflower. Volume and fiber for satiety. - **1/4 plate — Lean protein** — Chicken, fish, tofu, eggs, legumes. Palm-sized portion (~20–30 g protein). - **1/4 plate — Complex carbs** — Brown rice, sweet potato, quinoa, whole-grain bread. Fist-sized portion. - **Thumb of healthy fat** — Olive oil drizzle, avocado slice, handful of nuts.
## Benefits - No calorie counting or food scales required - Works at restaurants and social meals - Naturally portion-controlled (~500–600 kcal per plate) - Easy to teach to families and children
## Adjustments - **Weight loss** — Increase vegetables to 2/3, reduce carb portion - **Athletes** — Increase carb and protein portions proportionally - **Breakfast** — Same principle: eggs (protein) + oats (carb) + berries (veg/fruit) + nut butter (fat)

field.name: batch-prep
field.text:
# Batch Prep
Dedicate 2–3 hours once or twice a week to prepare components that mix-and-match into complete meals.
## Core Components to Prep 1. **2–3 proteins** — Grill chicken, bake salmon, cook a pot of lentils 2. **2–3 grains/starches** — Rice, quinoa, roasted sweet potatoes 3. **3–4 vegetables** — Roast a sheet pan of mixed veg, prep raw salad ingredients, steam broccoli 4. **Sauces/dressings** — Tahini dressing, chimichurri, simple vinaigrette
## Storage Guidelines - **Fridge** — Cooked proteins and grains last 3–4 days. Store in airtight containers. - **Freezer** — Soups, stews, and cooked grains freeze well for 2–3 months. Portion into single servings. - **Fresh components** — Wash and chop vegetables, but dress salads only when serving.
## Weekly Template | Day | Lunch | Dinner | |-----|-------|--------| | Mon | Chicken + quinoa + roasted veg | Salmon + rice + steamed broccoli | | Tue | Lentil bowl + sweet potato | Chicken stir-fry + rice | | Wed | Salmon salad + quinoa | Lentil soup (from freezer) | | Thu | Chicken wrap + raw veg | Fresh cook night | | Fri | Leftovers bowl | Flexible / eat out |
## Tips - Invest in quality glass containers (different sizes) - Label everything with date - Prep while something is in the oven — multitask

field.name: calorie-tracking
field.text:
# Calorie Tracking
A quantitative approach to nutrition — set daily calorie and macronutrient targets, then track intake against them.
## Setting Your Target 1. **Calculate TDEE** (Total Daily Energy Expenditure): - BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) x Activity Multiplier - Sedentary: BMR x 1.2 | Lightly active: x 1.375 | Active: x 1.55 | Very active: x 1.725 2. **Adjust for goal**: - **Fat loss** — Subtract 300–500 kcal (moderate deficit) - **Maintenance** — Eat at TDEE - **Muscle gain** — Add 200–300 kcal (lean bulk)
## Macro Split (Starting Point) - **Balanced** — 30% protein / 40% carbs / 30% fat - **High protein** — 35% protein / 35% carbs / 30% fat - **Endurance** — 25% protein / 50% carbs / 25% fat
## Practical Tips - Weigh food raw (cooked weight varies) - Front-load protein — plan protein sources first, fill in carbs and fats - Don't aim for perfection — within 50–100 kcal of target is fine - Track for 2–4 weeks to build intuition, then transition to intuitive eating if desired
## Common Pitfalls - Forgetting cooking oils (1 tbsp = 120 kcal) - Ignoring liquid calories (lattes, juice, alcohol) - Weekend "cheat days" that erase the weekly deficit - Obsessive tracking leading to disordered eating — take breaks if it feels unhealthy

entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

field.name: content
content
field.text:
← stop { knowledge: { "cooking": { "cuisine": { "type": { "japanese": "# Japanese Cuisine

Japanese cooking is defined by precision, respect for ingredients, and the pursuit of umami — the fifth taste.

## Key Principles
- **Umami layering** — Build depth by combining dashi, soy sauce, miso, and mirin in careful ratios.
- **Shun (旬)** — Cook with ingredients at their seasonal peak. The menu follows the calendar.
- **Presentation** — Food is arranged with intention. Odd numbers, negative space, and color contrast are valued.
- **Knife work** — Clean, precise cuts affect texture, cooking time, and appearance equally.

## Essential Techniques
- **Dashi making** — Steep kombu and bonito flakes briefly for a clean, umami-rich stock. Never boil the kombu.
- **Tempura frying** — Ice-cold batter, high heat (170–180°C), small batches. Lumps in the batter are fine — overmixing makes it heavy.
- **Rice washing** — Rinse until the water runs clear, then soak 30 minutes before cooking.
- **Teriyaki glaze** — Equal parts soy sauce, mirin, and sake, reduced until glossy.

## Staple Ingredients
Short-grain rice, soy sauce, mirin, sake, rice vinegar, miso paste, kombu, bonito flakes, nori, sesame oil, wasabi, ginger.

## Classic Dishes
Sushi, ramen, tempura, tonkatsu, miso soup, okonomiyaki, gyudon, matcha desserts." } }, "dietary": { "restriction": { "vegetarian": "# Vegetarian Cooking

Vegetarian cooking excludes meat and fish but includes dairy, eggs, and honey.

## Substitution Strategies
- **Protein sources** — Eggs, cheese, legumes (lentils, chickpeas, black beans), tofu, tempeh, seitan, nuts
- **Umami replacements** — Soy sauce, miso paste, nutritional yeast, aged cheeses, mushrooms, tomato paste
- **Stock** — Use vegetable stock or mushroom stock instead of chicken/beef stock
- **Gelatin** — Replace with agar-agar (1:1 ratio, but boil to activate)

## Tips
- Roast vegetables at high heat (220°C+) for caramelization and depth
- Use smoked paprika, liquid smoke, or charred vegetables for smoky notes usually provided by meat
- Don't treat vegetarian food as "meat minus the meat" — build dishes around vegetables as the star" } }, "technique": { "method": { "saute": "# Sauté

High-heat, quick cooking in a wide pan with a small amount of fat. The word comes from French "sauter" — to jump.

## When to Use
- Vegetables that should stay crisp-tender (bell peppers, snap peas, asparagus)
- Thin cuts of meat or fish (chicken cutlets, shrimp, scallops)
- Finishing a dish with a pan sauce after searing

## Key Technique
1. **Heat the pan first**, then add oil. The oil should shimmer but not smoke.
2. **Don't overcrowd** — ingredients should sit in a single layer with space between them. Work in batches if needed.
3. **Keep things moving** — toss or stir frequently. The goal is even browning without burning.
4. **Season at the end** — salt draws out moisture and can prevent browning.

## Equipment
A wide, flat-bottomed skillet or sauté pan. Stainless steel gives the best fond (browned bits) for pan sauces. Non-stick works for delicate items like fish.

## Common Mistakes
- Starting with a cold pan (food steams instead of searing)
- Adding too much food at once (temperature drops, food boils)
- Moving food too often when you want a sear (let it sit for 2–3 minutes)" } } }, "nutrition": { "macronutrients": { "type": { "protein": "# Protein

Protein is made up of amino acids — 9 of which are essential and must come from food.

## Daily Targets
- **Sedentary adults** — 0.8 g per kg of body weight
- **Active individuals** — 1.2–1.6 g/kg
- **Strength athletes** — 1.6–2.2 g/kg

## Complete vs Incomplete
- **Complete proteins** contain all 9 essential amino acids: meat, fish, eggs, dairy, soy, quinoa.
- **Incomplete proteins** lack one or more: beans, lentils, grains, nuts. Combine throughout the day to cover all amino acids.

## Top Sources (per 100 g)
| Food | Protein |
|------|---------|
| Chicken breast | 31 g |
| Greek yogurt | 10 g |
| Lentils (cooked) | 9 g |
| Eggs (2 large) | 12 g |
| Tofu (firm) | 17 g |

## Timing
Spreading protein across 3–4 meals (20–40 g each) maximizes muscle protein synthesis better than loading it into one meal.", "carbohydrates": "# Carbohydrates

Carbohydrates are the body's preferred fuel source, especially for the brain and high-intensity exercise.

## Types
- **Simple carbs** — Quick-digesting sugars (glucose, fructose, sucrose). Found in fruit, honey, table sugar.
- **Complex carbs** — Longer chains that digest slowly, providing steady energy. Found in whole grains, legumes, starchy vegetables.
- **Fiber** — Indigestible carbs that support gut health and satiety. Soluble fiber (oats, beans) lowers cholesterol; insoluble fiber (whole wheat, vegetables) aids digestion.

## Daily Targets
- **General** — 45–65% of total calories (225–325 g on a 2000 kcal diet)
- **Endurance athletes** — 6–10 g/kg body weight
- **Low-carb approaches** — 50–150 g/day depending on the protocol

## Glycemic Index
- **Low GI (< 55)** — Lentils, most fruits, steel-cut oats. Steady blood sugar.
- **Medium GI (56–69)** — Brown rice, whole wheat bread, sweet potato.
- **High GI (> 70)** — White bread, white rice, potatoes. Fast energy but rapid blood sugar spike.

## Top Sources
Oats, brown rice, sweet potatoes, quinoa, beans, whole-grain bread, bananas, berries.", "fats": "# Fats

Dietary fat is calorie-dense (9 kcal/g vs 4 kcal/g for protein and carbs) but essential for hormone production, brain function, and absorbing fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).

## Types
- **Unsaturated (healthy)** — Monounsaturated (olive oil, avocado) and polyunsaturated (fish, walnuts, flaxseed). Lower inflammation and support heart health.
- **Saturated** — Solid at room temperature (butter, coconut oil, red meat fat). Limit to < 10% of calories.
- **Trans fats** — Industrially hydrogenated oils. Avoid entirely — linked to heart disease.
- **Omega-3 fatty acids** — EPA and DHA (fatty fish) and ALA (flaxseed, chia). Anti-inflammatory, critical for brain health. Aim for 2 servings of fatty fish per week.

## Daily Targets
- **General** — 20–35% of total calories (44–78 g on a 2000 kcal diet)
- **Minimum** — Do not go below 15% of calories; hormonal disruption can occur.

## Top Sources
Olive oil, avocado, salmon, sardines, walnuts, almonds, chia seeds, eggs, dark chocolate (70%+)." } }, "meal-planning": { "strategy": { "plate-method": "# The Plate Method

A simple, no-counting approach to balanced meals. Divide your plate visually:

## The Template
- **1/2 plate — Non-starchy vegetables** — Leafy greens, broccoli, peppers, tomatoes, zucchini, cauliflower. Volume and fiber for satiety.
- **1/4 plate — Lean protein** — Chicken, fish, tofu, eggs, legumes. Palm-sized portion (~20–30 g protein).
- **1/4 plate — Complex carbs** — Brown rice, sweet potato, quinoa, whole-grain bread. Fist-sized portion.
- **Thumb of healthy fat** — Olive oil drizzle, avocado slice, handful of nuts.

## Benefits
- No calorie counting or food scales required
- Works at restaurants and social meals
- Naturally portion-controlled (~500–600 kcal per plate)
- Easy to teach to families and children

## Adjustments
- **Weight loss** — Increase vegetables to 2/3, reduce carb portion
- **Athletes** — Increase carb and protein portions proportionally
- **Breakfast** — Same principle: eggs (protein) + oats (carb) + berries (veg/fruit) + nut butter (fat)", "batch-prep": "# Batch Prep

Dedicate 2–3 hours once or twice a week to prepare components that mix-and-match into complete meals.

## Core Components to Prep
1. **2–3 proteins** — Grill chicken, bake salmon, cook a pot of lentils
2. **2–3 grains/starches** — Rice, quinoa, roasted sweet potatoes
3. **3–4 vegetables** — Roast a sheet pan of mixed veg, prep raw salad ingredients, steam broccoli
4. **Sauces/dressings** — Tahini dressing, chimichurri, simple vinaigrette

## Storage Guidelines
- **Fridge** — Cooked proteins and grains last 3–4 days. Store in airtight containers.
- **Freezer** — Soups, stews, and cooked grains freeze well for 2–3 months. Portion into single servings.
- **Fresh components** — Wash and chop vegetables, but dress salads only when serving.

## Weekly Template
| Day | Lunch | Dinner |
|-----|-------|--------|
| Mon | Chicken + quinoa + roasted veg | Salmon + rice + steamed broccoli |
| Tue | Lentil bowl + sweet potato | Chicken stir-fry + rice |
| Wed | Salmon salad + quinoa | Lentil soup (from freezer) |
| Thu | Chicken wrap + raw veg | Fresh cook night |
| Fri | Leftovers bowl | Flexible / eat out |

## Tips
- Invest in quality glass containers (different sizes)
- Label everything with date
- Prep while something is in the oven — multitask", "calorie-tracking": "# Calorie Tracking

A quantitative approach to nutrition — set daily calorie and macronutrient targets, then track intake against them.

## Setting Your Target
1. **Calculate TDEE** (Total Daily Energy Expenditure):
 - BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) x Activity Multiplier
 - Sedentary: BMR x 1.2 | Lightly active: x 1.375 | Active: x 1.55 | Very active: x 1.725
2. **Adjust for goal**:
 - **Fat loss** — Subtract 300–500 kcal (moderate deficit)
 - **Maintenance** — Eat at TDEE
 - **Muscle gain** — Add 200–300 kcal (lean bulk)

## Macro Split (Starting Point)
- **Balanced** — 30% protein / 40% carbs / 30% fat
- **High protein** — 35% protein / 35% carbs / 30% fat
- **Endurance** — 25% protein / 50% carbs / 25% fat

## Practical Tips
- Weigh food raw (cooked weight varies)
- Front-load protein — plan protein sources first, fill in carbs and fats
- Don't aim for perfection — within 50–100 kcal of target is fine
- Track for 2–4 weeks to build intuition, then transition to intuitive eating if desired

## Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting cooking oils (1 tbsp = 120 kcal)
- Ignoring liquid calories (lattes, juice, alcohol)
- Weekend "cheat days" that erase the weekly deficit
- Obsessive tracking leading to disordered eating — take breaks if it feels unhealthy" } } } } }

field.name: lines
field.text: "1 block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed",

field.name: lines
field.text: "Cook rice according to package directions (usually 1:1.5 rice:water ratio)",

field.name: lines
field.text: "Press tofu for 15 minutes to remove excess water, then cube",

field.name: lines
field.text: "Mix soy sauce, mirin, and sesame oil for the sauce",

field.name: lines
field.text: "Heat vegetable oil in a large skillet over high heat",

field.name: lines
field.text: "Sauté tofu cubes until golden brown on all sides, remove and set aside",

field.name: lines
field.text: "In the same pan, sauté garlic and ginger for 30 seconds",

field.name: lines
field.text: "Add bell pepper and snap peas, sauté until crisp-tender",

field.name: lines
field.text: "Return tofu to the pan, add sauce, and toss to coat",

field.name: lines
field.text: "Serve over rice, garnish with green onions and sesame seeds"

item.text: 1 block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed
Cook rice according to package directions (usually 1:1.5 rice:water ratio)
Press tofu for 15 minutes to remove excess water, then cube
Mix soy sauce, mirin, and sesame oil for the sauce
Heat vegetable oil in a large skillet over high heat
Sauté tofu cubes until golden brown on all sides, remove and set aside
In the same pan, sauté garlic and ginger for 30 seconds
Add mushrooms and sauté until softened
Add bell pepper and snap peas, sauté until crisp-tender
Return tofu to the pan, add sauce, and toss to coat
Serve over rice, garnish with green onions and sesame seeds
1.25 block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed

entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

field.name: content
content
field.text: ← stop { arg_0: { "baseRecipe": { "name": "Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl", "ingredients": ["2 cups short-grain rice", "1 block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed", "2 tbsp soy sauce", "1 tbsp mirin", "1 tsp sesame oil", "2 cups mixed mushrooms (shiitake, button), sliced", "1 bell pepper, julienned", "1 cup snap peas", "3 green onions, chopped", "2 cloves garlic, minced", "1 tsp fresh ginger, grated", "1 tbsp vegetable oil for sautéing", "Sesame seeds for garnish"], "steps": ["Cook rice according to package directions (usually 1:1.5 rice:water ratio)", "Press tofu for 15 minutes to remove excess water, then cube", "Mix soy sauce, mirin, and sesame oil for the sauce", "Heat vegetable oil in a large skillet over high heat", "Sauté tofu cubes until golden brown on all sides, remove and set aside", "In the same pan, sauté garlic and ginger for 30 seconds", "Add mushrooms and sauté until softened", "Add bell pepper and snap peas, sauté until crisp-tender", "Return tofu to the pan, add sauce, and toss to coat", "Serve over rice, garnish with green onions and sesame seeds"], "servings": 4, "cuisine": "japanese", "method": "saute" }, "scaledIngredients": ["2.5 cups short-grain rice", "1.25 block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed", "2.5 tbsp soy sauce", "1.25 tbsp mirin", "1.25 tsp sesame oil", "2.5 cups mixed mushrooms (shiitake, button), sliced", "1.25 bell pepper, julienned", "1.25 cup snap peas", "3.75 green onions, chopped", "2.5 cloves garlic, minced", "1.25 tsp fresh ginger, grated", "1.25 tbsp vegetable oil for sautéing", "Sesame seeds for garnish"] } }

item.text: 1 block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed
Cook rice according to package directions (usually 1:1.5 rice:water ratio)
Press tofu for 15 minutes to remove excess water, then cube
Mix soy sauce, mirin, and sesame oil for the sauce
Heat vegetable oil in a large skillet over high heat
Sauté tofu cubes until golden brown on all sides, remove and set aside
In the same pan, sauté garlic and ginger for 30 seconds
Add mushrooms and sauté until softened
Add bell pepper and snap peas, sauté until crisp-tender
Return tofu to the pan, add sauce, and toss to coat
Serve over rice, garnish with green onions and sesame seeds

entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

field.name: content
item
field.text: block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed

entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

field.name: content
content
field.text: ← stop { groceryList: [{ "item": "cups short-grain rice", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "tbsp soy sauce", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "tbsp mirin", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "tsp sesame oil", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "cups mixed mushrooms (shiitake, button), sliced", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "bell pepper, julienned", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "cup snap peas", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "green onions, chopped", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "cloves garlic, minced", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "tsp fresh ginger, grated", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "tbsp vegetable oil for sautéing", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "sesame seeds for garnish", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }] }

field.name: item
field.text: block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed

item.text: 1.25 block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed
Cook rice according to package directions (usually 1:1.5 rice:water ratio)
Press tofu for 15 minutes to remove excess water, then cube
Mix soy sauce, mirin, and sesame oil for the sauce
Heat vegetable oil in a large skillet over high heat
Sauté tofu cubes until golden brown on all sides, remove and set aside
In the same pan, sauté garlic and ginger for 30 seconds
Add mushrooms and sauté until softened
Add bell pepper and snap peas, sauté until crisp-tender
Return tofu to the pan, add sauce, and toss to coat
Serve over rice, garnish with green onions and sesame seeds

field.name: lines
field.text: content="High heat, quick cooking preserves the crisp texture of vegetables. Keep ingredients moving in the pan to prevent burning. Sauté aromatics first to release flavors into the oil."

item.text: High heat, quick cooking preserves the crisp texture of vegetables. Keep ingredients moving in the pan to prevent burning. Sauté aromatics first to release flavors into the oil.

field.name: lines
field.text: description: "Rice (50% of plate) - provides carbohydrates for energy"

field.name: lines
field.text: description: "Tofu (25% of plate) - complete protein with all essential amino acids"

field.name: lines
field.text: description: "Mixed vegetables (25% of plate) - fiber, vitamins, and minerals"

item.text: Rice (50% of plate) - provides carbohydrates for energy
Tofu (25% of plate) - complete protein with all essential amino acids
Mixed vegetables (25% of plate) - fiber, vitamins, and minerals

field.name: description
field.text: Plan and prepare a meal for 5 people using all available functions

field.name: instructions
field.text: Ask user about cuisine, dietary restrictions, and cooking preferences

field.name: instructions
field.text: Load relevant knowledge from cooking and nutrition spaces

field.name: instructions
field.text: Create a recipe proposal for 5 people

field.name: instructions
field.text: Calculate nutrition information for the meal

field.name: instructions
field.text: Build a grocery list for the meal

field.name: instructions
field.text: Display all results using display components

item.text: 1 block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed
Cook rice according to package directions (usually 1:1.5 rice:water ratio)
Press tofu for 15 minutes to remove excess water, then cube
Mix soy sauce, mirin, and sesame oil for the sauce
Heat vegetable oil in a large skillet over high heat
Sauté tofu cubes until golden brown on all sides, remove and set aside
In the same pan, sauté garlic and ginger for 30 seconds
Add mushrooms and sauté until softened
Add bell pepper and snap peas, sauté until crisp-tender
Return tofu to the pan, add sauce, and toss to coat
Serve over rice, garnish with green onions and sesame seeds

field.name: item
field.text: block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed

field.name: content
field.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

field.name: content
field.text: lets cook something for 5 persons. Ask me questions and use all the available functions to propose

field.name: content
field.text: checkpoints("cooking_for_5", "Plan and prepare a meal for 5 people using all available functions", [ { id: "gather_preferences", instructions: "Ask user about cuisine, dietary restrictions, and cooking preferences", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "load_knowledge", instructions: "Load relevant knowledge from cooking and nutrition spaces", outputSchema: { loaded: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "create_recipe", instructions: "Create a recipe proposal for 5 people", outputSchema: { recipe: { type: "object" } } }, { id: "calculate_nutrition", instructions: "Calculate nutrition information for the meal", outputSchema: { nutrition: { type: "object" } } }, { id: "build_grocery_list", instructions: "Build a grocery list for the meal", outputSchema: { groceries: { type: "array" } } }, { id: "present_results", instructions: "Display all results using display components", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ]) var preferences = await ask(

field.name: content
content
field.text: ← stop { knowledge: { "cooking": { "cuisine": { "type": { "japanese": "# Japanese Cuisine | ## Key Principles | ## Essential Techniques | ## Staple Ingredients | ## Classic Dishes" } }, "dietary": { "restriction": { "vegetarian": "# Vegetarian Cooking | ## Substitution Strategies | ## Tips" } }, "technique": { "method": { "saute": "# Sauté | ## When to Use | ## Key Technique | ## Equipment | ## Common Mistakes" } } }, "nutrition": { "macronutrients": { "type": { "protein": "# Protein | ## Daily Targets | ## Complete vs Incomplete | ## Top Sources (per 100 g) | ## Timing", "carbohydrates": "# Carbohydrates | ## Types | ## Daily Targets | ## Glycemic Index | ## Top Sources", "fats": "# Fats | ## Types | ## Daily Targets | ## Top Sources" } }, "meal-planning": { "strategy": { "plate-method": "# The Plate Method | ## The Template | ## Benefits | ## Adjustments", "batch-prep": "# Batch Prep | ## Core Components to Prep | ## Storage Guidelines | ## Weekly Template | ## Tips", "calorie-tracking": "# Calorie Tracking | ## Setting Your Target | ## Macro Split (Starting Point) | ## Practical Tips | ## Common Pitfalls" } } } } }

field.name: content
content
field.text: ← stop { arg_0: { "baseRecipe": { "name": "Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl", "ingredients": ["2 cups short-grain rice", "1 block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed", "2 tbsp soy sauce", "1 tbsp mirin", "1 tsp sesame oil", "2 cups mixed mushrooms (shiitake, button), sliced", "1 bell pepper, julienned", "1 cup snap peas", "3 green onions, chopped", "2 cloves garlic, minced", "1 tsp fresh ginger, grated", "1 tbsp vegetable oil for sautéing", "Sesame seeds for garnish"], "steps": ["Cook rice according to package directions (usually 1:1.5 rice:water ratio)", "Press tofu for 15 minutes to remove excess water, then cube", "Mix soy sauce, mirin, and sesame oil for the sauce", "Heat vegetable oil in a large skillet over high heat", "Sauté tofu cubes until golden brown on all sides, remove and set aside", "In the same pan, sauté garlic and ginger for 30 seconds", "Add mushrooms and sauté until softened", "Add bell pepper and snap peas, sauté until crisp-tender", "Return tofu to the pan, add sauce, and toss to coat", "Serve over rice, garnish with green onions and sesame seeds"], "servings": 4, "cuisine": "japanese", "method": "saute" }, "scaledIngredients": ["2.5 cups short-grain rice", "1.25 block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed", "2.5 tbsp soy sauce", "1.25 tbsp mirin", "1.25 tsp sesame oil", "2.5 cups mixed mushrooms (shiitake, button), sliced", "1.25 bell pepper, julienned", "1.25 cup snap peas", "3.75 green onions, chopped", "2.5 cloves garlic, minced", "1.25 tsp fresh ginger, grated", "1.25 tbsp vegetable oil for sautéing", "Sesame seeds for garnish"] } }

field.name: content
content
content
field.text: ← stop { groceryList: [{ "item": "cups short-grain rice", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "block firm tofu (14oz), pressed and cubed", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "tbsp soy sauce", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "tbsp mirin", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "tsp sesame oil", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "cups mixed mushrooms (shiitake, button), sliced", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "bell pepper, julienned", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "cup snap peas", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "green onions, chopped", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "cloves garlic, minced", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "tsp fresh ginger, grated", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "tbsp vegetable oil for sautéing", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }, { "item": "sesame seeds for garnish", "recipes": ["Vegetarian Japanese Sauté Bowl"] }] }
